system_description.overview: Vimes INC provides crisis intervention software for law enforcement, child welfare agencies, schools, and community organizations to triage cases of child abuse and domestic violence. The platform leverages AI and advanced workflows to connect mandatory reporters, automate referrals, and enable secure data sharing to reduce child fatalities. It is accessible on all device types as a cloud-based SaaS product.

products.Vimes Platform: Secure software system for data communication, reporting, analysis, risk assessments, behavioral threat assessments, and automated notifications for mandatory reporters.
products.Automated Referral System: Automatically sends DFCS, child welfare, and child advocacy center referrals as needed.
products.School Safety Reporting System: Enables instantaneous connection between schools, law enforcement, and child welfare; provides access to floor plans, crisis management software, and anonymous threat assessment reports.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Vercel (declared subservice, carve-out method); actual production architecture depicted in network diagram shows AWS (AWS Fargate, DynamoDB, S3, ElasticSearch, Cognito, SES, WAF, GuardDuty, Secrets Manager, ELB, Auto Scaling)
regions: Multi-region (two regions shown in network diagram with active/inactive failover designation — specific region names not disclosed)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud services
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (multiple availability zones per region shown in diagram; daily backups confirm multi-AZ configuration)
serverless: AWS Fargate (container-based serverless compute shown in diagram)
infrastructure.cdn: Not explicitly mentioned; Vercel platform includes CDN by default

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Vercel in a VPC environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network diagram shows two VPCs across two regions, each with multiple availability zones and separate subnets.
network_architecture.waf: AWS WAF (shown at top of network diagram as entry point)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via Vercel/AWS (Amazon GuardDuty shown in network diagram); firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external threats.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS (Transport Layer Security) using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet with content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Traffic enters through AWS WAF, passes through Amazon GuardDuty, then routes to active or inactive region. Each region has a VPC with multiple availability zones, Elastic Load Balancing, Auto Scaling groups, and AWS Fargate compute. Amazon Cognito handles OIDC authentication with cross-region Identity Sync. Data tier includes Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon S3, Amazon ElasticSearch Service, Amazon Simple Email Service, and AWS Secrets Manager. Multi-region replica and async cross-region replication (DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 Cross-Region Replication) provide DR capability.

application_architecture.pattern: Microservices (AWS Fargate containers, multiple AWS managed services per diagram)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4 confirmed); changes tested before implementation

databases: Amazon DynamoDB (DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-region replication shown in diagram)
file_storage: Amazon S3 (with S3 Cross-Region Replication shown in diagram)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (CC7.4.2: 'Backups are taken daily, multi-availability zones are configured')

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Amazon Cognito (shown in network diagram for OIDC authentication with cross-region Identity Sync); password policy managed through Vercel
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required for employees to access email; MFA required to access source code repository
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Passwords controlled through Vercel password policy; includes periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; user accounts disabled after limited number of unsuccessful logon attempts; guest/anonymous logins not allowed

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service; access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Git-based source code management tool (branch protection rules configured; MFA required for access; specific tool — GitHub/GitLab — not named)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to move change to production environment (CC8.1.2/CC8.1.3)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations — retained as code, used for implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1); specific tool not named
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: AWS Fargate (ECS-based serverless containers per network diagram)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO has set up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews performed; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool generates alerts when predefined thresholds are met; monitors CPU, disk space, incoming/outgoing network traffic, packet loss)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not named (logging enabled at application and infrastructure level; access to modify logs restricted)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; logs and alerts tracked until resolved in change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Declared subservice organization for data center/cloud services; carve-out method — Vercel's SOC 2 reviewed annually
Actual production infrastructure provider per network diagram: WAF, GuardDuty, Fargate, DynamoDB, S3, ElasticSearch, Cognito, SES, Secrets Manager, ELB, Auto Scaling, CloudBatch
Email delivery service (shown in network diagram)
Identity and OIDC authentication with cross-region sync

security_tools.antivirus: Endpoint Security Solution installed on all desktops and laptops; antivirus, anti-malware, Trojan protection; AV definitions updated weekly for production servers, daily from vendor for workstations; Mac-based workstations and laptops covered
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; self-assessment scan performed prior to each production release or annually; internal IT Infrastructure team performs periodic scans
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party (CC4.1.1); high/critical findings remediated and tracked to resolution

untestable: CC2.2.3 — no significant changes to key personnel roles/responsibilities during the engagement period
CC3.2.1 (cybersecurity insurance) — no cyber incidents reported during the engagement period
CC6.5.2 — no customer terminations during the engagement period
CC7.3.1 — no security incidents reported during the engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Vercel is responsible for physical security of data center; not in scope for Vimes
A1.2 — Vimes does not have an office; all environmental security controls managed by building management/Vercel
Processing Integrity trust criteria — determined not relevant to the system
Privacy trust criteria — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC7.1.1: Baseline configurations retained as infrastructure-as-code with rollback capability
CC8.1.2: Code repository branch protection rules enforce approval for all merges to master
CC8.1.3: Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed; MFA required for source code tool access
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
CC7.4.2: Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed by auditor inspection of onscreen evidence
CC3.1.1: IT Leadership Committee meets weekly; executive team meets weekly on security/operations
CC2.2.4: Security awareness training required at hire and at least annually

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-region active/inactive failover, multi-AZ per region, AWS WAF, GuardDuty, Fargate containers, IaC with rollback, async cross-region replication for DynamoDB and S3. Strong architecture for a small company. Loses points because specific region names are not disclosed and the Vercel-as-subservice framing creates opacity about actual AWS account ownership.

application_architecture.rationale: Container-based (Fargate) with separate environments and branch protection — solid fundamentals. No languages, frameworks, or specific CI/CD tooling named. No detail on API design, testing practices, or observability tooling. Moderate disclosure.

data_layer.rationale: DynamoDB with Global Tables (multi-region), S3 with cross-region replication, ElasticSearch, daily backups confirmed by auditor inspection. Data classification and retention policies in place. Missing: no caching layer mentioned, no data warehouse, no explicit multi-tenant isolation detail beyond per-customer account segmentation.

security_posture.rationale: AWS WAF + GuardDuty (IDS) + firewalls + TLS + KMS encryption + MFA on admin and source code + annual external pen test + monthly vuln scans + pre-release vuln scans + endpoint protection + cybersecurity insurance + quarterly access reviews + 1-day access revocation SLA. Comprehensive for a startup. No SIEM named, monitoring tool not named.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC confirmed (configuration as code with rollback), branch protection with mandatory approval, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch reviews, Fargate containers. However, CI/CD tool not named, no deployment frequency or DORA metrics, source code tool unnamed.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually, annual DR testing confirmed (loss scenarios tested at least annually), multi-AZ and multi-region failover via Vercel/AWS AZ spin-up, daily backups confirmed by auditor. Significant gap: no RTO/RPO stated anywhere in the report.

vendor_diversity.rationale: AWS is effectively the sole infrastructure provider (despite Vercel being named as subservice, diagram shows AWS services end-to-end). This creates heavy single-vendor concentration on AWS. Vercel may be an abstraction layer on AWS. No named monitoring, SIEM, CI/CD, or ticketing vendors. Only 1 explicit subservice org reviewed.

overall.rationale: Vimes demonstrates a mature security and infrastructure posture for an early-stage startup in a sensitive vertical (child abuse/domestic violence data). The network diagram reveals sophisticated AWS architecture with multi-region failover, GuardDuty, WAF, and cross-region replication. Clean SOC 2 with zero exceptions and only untestable (not failed) controls. Main risks: AWS concentration, no RTO/RPO disclosed, Vercel carve-out limits auditor scope over the actual infrastructure, and no named monitoring/SIEM/CI-CD tooling creates opacity.

red_flags: The report names Vercel as the sole subservice organization (carve-out), but the network diagram shows AWS-native services (Fargate, DynamoDB, GuardDuty, WAF, Cognito, S3, ElasticSearch, SES, Secrets Manager). This suggests Vimes runs on AWS directly — possibly via Vercel's infrastructure — but the auditor only carved out 'Vercel,' not AWS, creating a significant scope gap.
No RTO or RPO values disclosed anywhere in the 79-page report.
No specific CI/CD tool, monitoring tool, SIEM, ticketing system, or log aggregation tool named — makes it impossible to assess vendor risk for operational tooling.
Only a 3-month audit period (April 6 – July 6, 2025), which is unusually short for a Type 2 report and limits the evidence base for operating effectiveness.
yellow_flags: Source code management tool not named (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket unknown); MFA confirmed but vendor risk unassessed.
No caching layer mentioned (Redis/ElastiCache) despite DynamoDB usage — may indicate performance architecture gaps or simply undisclosed.
Amazon CloudBatch shown in network diagram but not described in system description — purpose unclear.
Penetration test frequency is annual; no bug bounty program mentioned.
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded — notable for a platform handling sensitive child abuse case data.
Cybersecurity insurance confirmed but could not be tested (no incidents) — coverage limits not disclosed.
Legal name in report says 'Vimes INC' while company_db has 'Vimes LLC' — entity type discrepancy worth verifying.
green_flags: Multi-region active/inactive failover with async cross-region replication (DynamoDB Global Tables + S3 CRR) — strong for a startup.
AWS WAF + Amazon GuardDuty as IDS at network perimeter — sophisticated threat detection.
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed and tested by auditor.
Monthly automated vulnerability scans plus pre-release self-assessment scans.
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed via auditor inspection of onscreen evidence.
MFA enforced on production admin access, email, and source code repository access.
1-business-day access revocation SLA for terminated employees.
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts.
Infrastructure as code with rollback capability.
Branch protection with mandatory approval gates on all production merges.
Zero exceptions across all tested controls; 4 untestable controls all due to absence of triggering events (no incidents, no terminations, no personnel changes) — not control failures.
BCDR plans annually tested against multiple loss scenarios.
IT Leadership Committee meets weekly; executive team meets weekly on security and operations.
key_observations: There is a striking mismatch between the system description (which describes Vercel as the cloud provider) and the network diagram (which shows a full AWS architecture with 10+ AWS-native services). Vimes almost certainly runs on AWS, likely using Vercel as a deployment/edge platform that itself runs on AWS. The SOC 2 auditor carved out 'Vercel' but did not separately call out AWS, meaning the AWS infrastructure controls are uncovered by audit.

The network diagram shows two VPCs in two separate regions with an active/inactive designation — this is a proper active-passive DR architecture, which is unusually sophisticated for a startup with a 3-month-old SOC 2.
Amazon Cognito with OIDC and cross-region Identity Sync is shown in the diagram — this is the identity provider, not a named third-party IdP like Okta or Auth0.

The platform handles highly sensitive data (child abuse cases, domestic violence, mandatory reporter information, school floor plans) but Privacy trust criteria was explicitly excluded from scope. This is a notable gap for prospective enterprise customers in regulated environments (HIPAA, FERPA, state child welfare regulations).

The signing authority is Kevin Zhu (CTO & Co-Founder), dated 8/9/2025 — the same date as the management assertion but after the July 6 audit period end, suggesting this was Kevin Zhu's first SOC 2 report as CTO.

The auditor license number PAC-FIRM-LIC-47383 appears on page 7 without a firm name beyond 'Accorp Partners' — Accorp Partners is a known SOC 2 mill that produces templated reports. The control descriptions are heavily templated and many controls reuse identical language across multiple criteria.
